papers.title: AE Aurigae: first detection of non-thermal X-ray emission from a bow shock produced by a runaway star
papers.author_names: J. López-Santiago M. Miceli M. V. del Valle G. E. Romero R. Bonito J. F. Albacete-Colombo V. Pereira E. de Castro F. Damiani

abstract.text: Runaway stars produce shocks when passing through interstellar medium at supersonic velocities. Bow shocks have been detected in the mid-infrared for several high-mass runaway stars and in radio waves for one star. Theoretical models predict the production of high-energy photons by non-thermal radiative processes in a number sufficiently large to be detected in X-rays. To date, no stellar bow shock has been detected at such energies. We present the first detection of X-ray emission from a bow shock produced by a runaway star. The star is AE Aur, which was likely expelled from its birthplace by the encounter of two massive binary systems and now is passing through the dense nebula IC 405. The X-ray emission from the bow shock is detected at 30" to the northeast of the star, coinciding with an enhancement in the density of the nebula. From the analysis of the observed X-ray spectrum of the source and our theoretical emission model, we confirm that the X-ray emission is produced mainly by inverse Compton upscattering of infrared photons from dust in the shock front.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The first observational detection of non-thermal X-ray emission from a bow shock produced by the runaway star AE Aur, located in the dense IC 405 nebula. X-ray emission at 30 arcsec northeast of AE Aur is attributed to inverse Compton scattering of infrared dust photons by relativistic electrons accelerated at the shock front, confirming theoretical predictions of high-energy particle acceleration in stellar bow shocks. The detection provides direct evidence for non-thermal radiative processes in interstellar medium interactions involving high-velocity runaway stars.

source_fields.title: AE Aurigae: first detection of non-thermal X-ray emission from a bow shock produced by a runaway star

source_fields.sections_text: Conclusion: This study reports the first detection of X-ray emission from a bow shock produced by a runaway star, AE Aur, confirming a non-thermal source linked to the infrared bow shock in IC 405 through a radiative model consistent with inverse Compton emission. Introduction: This paper makes the novel contribution of reporting the first detection of hard-energy emission from a bow shock associated with a runaway star, addressing the long-standing problem of unobserved high-energy radiation predicted by theoretical models. Non-Thermal Radiative Model: The paper proposes a non-thermal radiative model based on inverse Compton scattering of infrared photons by relativistic electrons accelerated via Fermi I process in the reverse shock of a runaway star's bow shock, which successfully reproduces the observed X-ray spectrum of AE Aur with a power-law electron injection index of −2.6. The Runaway Star AE Aur (HIP 24575): AE Aur, a runaway O9.5 star ejected from the Orion nebula cluster, produced a bow shock after encountering IC 405, with observable X-ray and mid-infrared emissions linked to its high velocity and strong stellar wind. X-ray Observation and Data Analysis: This section describes the X-ray observation and analysis of AE Aur, revealing a hard X-ray source embedded in its bow shock with non-thermal emission, detected through EPIC data analysis and spectral modeling.

abstract: Runaway stars produce shocks when passing through interstellar medium at supersonic velocities.
Bow shocks have been detected in the mid-infrared for several high-mass runaway stars and in radio waves for one star.
We present the first detection of X-ray emission from a bow shock produced by a runaway star.
title: AE Aurigae: first detection of non-thermal X-ray emission from a bow shock produced by a runaway star

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper presents first detection of X-ray emission from bow shock of runaway star AE Aur, with observational data including X-ray spectrum analysis and spatial location relative to star
